DB Strategic Advisory

Didier Breant

Senior Finance Executive · Board Director · Advisor

30 years building and leading governance, finance, risk, valuation, and operational infrastructure at Goldman Sachs and Eisler Capital. I now advise boards, founders, and institutional leadership teams that need senior judgement — not just a consultant's report. Available for NED appointments, strategic advisory, consulting, and fractional CFO/COO roles across alternative investment managers, investment banks, and the broader regulated financial sector.

Goldman Sachs — Managing Director, Product Control & Valuation, 20 years

About

Who I Am

I am a senior finance executive with 30 years of experience spanning Deloitte Paris, Goldman Sachs London (Managing Director, Product Control and Valuation), and Eisler Capital (CFO, CRO, and Senior Advisor). I hold dual Masters degrees in Finance (ESSEC) and Engineering (École Centrale de Lille) — a combination that shaped how I think: evidence-first, rigorous, and commercial in equal measure.

My career has been built at the intersection of governance, control, and commercial reality. At Goldman Sachs, I led global Product Control across the most complex trading businesses — Rates, FX, Emerging Markets, and Macro Prop — and became the firm’s recognised Subject Matter Expert in derivatives valuation. I championed the adoption of OIS discounting and FVA, positioning Goldman as a first mover and generating hundreds of millions in revenues and savings. That came from a control function.

At Eisler Capital, I joined at inception in 2016 and built the entire governance, finance, and risk infrastructure from scratch as the firm grew from $0.7bn AUM and 10 people at inception to $4.5bn AUM and 300+ people across multiple global locations. I held multiple mandates simultaneously — CFO, CRO, Senior Advisor, and Board Director across five jurisdictions — and contributed directly to the firm’s capital-raising effort through investor ODD presentations. Institutional investors consistently cited our governance as among the strongest they had seen.

Through DB Strategic Advisory, I bring that experience to boards, founders, and leadership teams that are building, scaling, or strengthening their institutions. My conviction is simple: governance means business. The firms that treat it as a strategic asset — rather than a compliance cost — are the ones that grow, attract institutional capital, and survive periods of stress.

View LinkedIn Profile
Career threads
Engineering mindset applied to financeEvidence, analysis, process rigour — always going deeper into the subject rather than accepting surface-level answers.
Commercial, not just conservativeControl functions that only say no are captured or ignored. The goal is to be fair — and to add genuine commercial value alongside the oversight.
The Federation principleFinance, risk, compliance, legal and operations are strategic partners to the front office — not subordinates to it.
Institutional then entrepreneurialBuilt career at Goldman Sachs, one of the world’s largest investment banks, then joined Eisler Capital at inception and helped scale it from a 10-person start-up to a 300+ person global platform. Each environment required a fundamentally different mode of operating — both demanded the same standard.
People developerStrong record of hiring, mentoring and promoting talent across UK, US, India, Tokyo and Hong Kong.
Global leadershipExtensive leadership across US, Europe and Asia — managing geographically distributed teams and navigating diverse regulatory and cultural environments.
Business optimisationConsistent track record of optimising business models and driving structural efficiency — across cost, tax, capital, and operational design.
Technology as a leverConsistently harnessed technology to scale operations and automate critical processes — from live intraday P&L to firm-wide expense management automation.
Strategic and operationalOperates at both strategic and execution level — sets long-term vision whilst driving near-term delivery. Comfortable building from scratch as much as advising at board level.
Services

What DB Strategic Advisory Offers

Four distinct engagement modes, each with a clear value proposition. Clients are hedge funds, alternative asset managers, emerging platforms, and regulated financial entities.

I
Non-Executive Director
Independent Board Membership

Independent NED appointments for regulated entities across multiple jurisdictions. A track record of board-level engagement with some of the world’s most demanding regulators — FCA, DFSA, SEC/NFA, MFSA, and JFSC — delivering substantive governance, not just regulatory compliance.

Value proposition
Credibility
FCA-approved SMF3 track record. Institutional investors running ODD treat the calibre of independent board members as a direct signal of governance quality — the right NED can be a fundraising asset.
Challenge
Willing and able to challenge management — built over decades of telling senior traders their positions were worth less than they believed, and being right.
Multi-jurisdiction
Direct regulatory engagement across UK, EU, Dubai, Jersey, and US. No ramp-up cost on regulatory expectations — familiar with how FCA, DFSA, SEC, MFSA and JFSC approach governance and senior accountability.
Commercial context
20 years at Goldman Sachs means board input grounded in how a world-class investment bank operates — not just in what the rulebook says.

II
Strategic Advisory
Senior Counsel for Leadership Teams

Ongoing advisory engagements for founders, CEOs and executive teams navigating governance transitions, operational scaling, regulatory change, or investor scrutiny. Independent, direct, and outcome-oriented — not a slideware exercise.

Value proposition
Pattern recognition
Having built and led governance at Goldman and Eisler, I recognise failure modes before they materialise — rather than diagnosing them after the fact.
Investor lens
ODD and IDD presentations across the full journey from $0.7bn to $4.5bn AUM at Eisler Capital. I know exactly what institutional investors are looking for — and where governance narratives break down.
Independence
Advisory roles are most valuable when the advisor is willing to be unpopular. I have challenged founders and senior partners throughout my career.
Breadth
CFO, CRO, and COO experience means advice that crosses function boundaries — not siloed input from a narrow specialism.
Typical advisory mandates
✓
Governance review and redesign ahead of institutional capital raise
✓
Operational due diligence preparation and investor presentation coaching
✓
Strategic review of control and finance function structures
✓
Advisory support through regulatory transition or new licence application
✓
Sounding board for founders and CEOs on governance, risk, and operational decisions
Suitable for
Founders and CEOsEmerging ManagersScaling PlatformsPre-ODD Firms
III
Consulting
Project-Based Expert Engagements

Defined-scope projects with clear deliverables. Deep technical expertise in governance framework design, valuation policy, fund accounting architecture, risk frameworks, AI governance, and operational optimisation. Practitioner-level design work — not generic advice.

Value proposition
Built, not advised
Every framework I consult on, I have built from the ground up — at Goldman or Eisler. Recommendations come from doing the work, not observing it.
Commercial impact
I championed Goldman Sachs’ first-mover adoption of OIS discounting and FVA on the Federation side, generating meaningful bottom-line impact for the firm. A proprietary pass-through expense platform drove full automation of expense management at scale. R&D tax relief. Regulatory capital optimisation. Governance work with a measurable bottom line.
AI governance
At Eisler Capital, we built a firm-wide AI governance policy — mapping regulatory baseline through to ownership structure. Published a practitioner framework covering FCA SM&CR, DFSA, and AIMA guidance.
Areas of deep expertise
✓
Governance framework design — policy through to committee structure and ownership
✓
Derivatives valuation policy and complex instrument governance (OTC derivatives, illiquid assets)
✓
Fund accounting architecture — valuation agent model, Shadow NAV, FA reconciliation
✓
Risk limit framework design — VaR, Greeks, PM-level and fund-level governance
✓
Pass-through expense platform design and automation
✓
AI governance policy for alternative investment managers
✓
Business optimisation — transfer pricing, R&D relief, accounting policy, margin optimisation

IV
Fractional CFO / COO
Institutional-Grade C-Suite on a Part-Time Basis

CFO or COO engagement on a fractional or interim basis. Suited to emerging managers, new platform launches, or firms in transition that need senior leadership without the cost of a full-time hire. Draws on 30 years of building and leading finance, risk, and governance functions at Goldman Sachs and Eisler Capital — including building Eisler from inception.

What I deliver — and the commercial case
Streamline and scale
Technology-led automation of support functions enables scaling without proportional headcount growth. In a pass-through structure, a 1bp saving in expenses equals 1bp more performance before performance fee.
Institutional control
A credible control environment is a prerequisite for allocations from pension funds, endowments, and sovereign wealth funds. $100m in additional AUM equals $2m in annual management fees (assuming a 2% fee) — governance that pays for itself.
Optimise
Tax (R&D relief, transfer pricing, compensation accounting), regulatory capital, business model structure. Rigorous review of tax structure, accounting policy, and capital deployment consistently uncovers value that generic approaches miss.
Stakeholders
Deep relationships with fund administrators, auditors, prime brokers, and counterparties — turning service providers into extensions of the team. Maintained all credit lines through market stress and reputational incidents.
Capital raising
Direct contribution to ODD/IDD success across $4.5bn of AUM growth. Consistently strong investor feedback on governance standards. Good governance is not just internally important — it opens doors.
What is included
✓
Weekly management reporting — P&L, cash, regulatory capital projections
✓
Valuation framework and fund accounting architecture
✓
External relationships — auditors, accountants, administrators, tax advisors
✓
Transfer pricing policy and multi-jurisdiction tax structure
✓
Compensation accounting framework design
✓

✓
ODD/IDD presentation and investor due diligence support
✓

Career

Professional Experience

Thirty years across audit, sell-side, and buy-side — each transition deepening rather than narrowing capability. Download full CV (PDF)

Education
ESSEC · ECL
June 2016 — November 2025 / 9 Years
Managing Director — Eisler Capital, London
Hedge Fund / Multi-strategy pass-through platform / $4.5bn AUM at peak

In 9 years, we grew AUM from $0.7bn to $4.5bn and achieved a peak annual return of 15%. The firm started as a Macro fund with 10 people in London and ended as a Passthrough Multi Strategy fund with 300+ people across multiple global locations.

I joined Eisler Capital at inception having worked with the founder for many years at Goldman Sachs.

Our trusting relationship enabled me to ensure Governance was implemented with the founder’s full support which increased Eisler’s credibility with external stakeholders.

I was driven to continually go further by engaging in new areas of the business and developing key processes that paid off in the longer term. I realised many skills are transferrable and rigour with method is crucial.

Throughout the years I wore multiple hats and I had to think both tactically and strategically. As we needed to get started I had to think “what/how can we do now with very little so that we can run/expand the business without compromising the need for control/governance?” However I always kept in mind the strategic perspective: “what is the long term path to a scalable business?”

I really enjoyed the learning journey and the various opportunities to stretch beyond my comfort zone. Ability to adapt and ask the right questions was critical to the success.

Senior Advisor — Board Director of the Operating Entities

I established an effective governance and oversight across a portfolio of operating entities.

The founder of the firm invited me to take an advisory role to ensure sound governance across the board and contribute to the firm’s external reputation. To contribute successfully, I was able to challenge decisions or proposals in order to trigger further thinking from Management. I also focused on creating a bridge between the firm and the other NEDs so that they could bring their expertise and relationships to bear.

Senior Advisor — Executive Committee Memberships

I was appointed to the firm’s 6 Executive Committees.

In that capacity I actively engaged senior management so that they became committed to the governance framework and could see beyond the legal/regulatory tick box requirements. My advisory role empowered me to express my views freely and independently. The role spanned across our governance framework: investment, risk, valuation, expense, conflict of interest, operational risk, etc.

CFO (“Chief Financial Officer”) — Corporate Finance

I focused on the optimization of the business (esp. tax, capital, dividend.) but also on building automation of the processes (and more particularly the passthrough reimbursement process).

I built the Corporate Finance function engaging with service providers such as Auditors, Accountants, banks, tax advisors. I managed to gather significant leverage keeping the core team lean. I also built weekly management reporting providing KPI such as performance, cash projection and capital requirements. I focused on technology to automate all our processes and in particular with regards to pass through expenses. With the help of our various service providers, I built a new compensation accounting framework to achieve better people retention, I also focused on establishing our tax framework across jurisdictions (e.g. Transfer Pricing) and leveraging from tax incentives (e.g. R&D).

CFO (“Chief Financial Officer”) — Fund Finance

I established a less common process with the Fund Administrators: Eisler would be the valuation agent of the portfolio and the Fund Administrator would independently verify these prices at month end. This was more suited to the complexity of our portfolio and mitigated the P&L discrepancies between the Manager and the Administrator which is an issue often found at other firms.

On the Fund Finance side of my role, I had the opportunity to build a robust Fund Accounting function from scratch. I particularly focused on our valuation framework given the complexity of the products we traded (OTC derivatives). As we built our technology, we progressively moved from a London close T+1 P&L to multi regional close T+0/live P&L. I also presented to existing/prospect investors at ODD/IDD meetings. I actively engaged with our fund administrators so that they would naturally become an extension of the Eisler team. I strongly believed that the more they understood our business, the better their output. I also onboarded new auditors and established a very valuable relationship.

CRO (“Chief Risk Officer”)

Building an effective risk framework required establishing measurements from scratch. The risk limit structure enabled portfolio managers (PMs) to generate alpha but with the appropriate oversight.

At inception of the firm I had to focus on setting up the system for risk. That involved generating price feeds, building Greeks, setting up VaR, etc. Then as we hired more PMs, I focused on building the Risk Management framework setting up Fund level and PM level limits. I quickly understood that a framework prompts PMs behaviour wrt risk/capital deployment. Hence, I valued PMs feedback and their experience from other firms so that I could enhance the approach. Also, as long as the number of PMs allowed it, I actively engaged with them so that sound judgement maybe applied when needed (e.g. cutting risk, increasing limits). Although Risk Management was outside my core background (Derivative valuation), I realised that applying common sense was key.

Additional responsibilities — Counterparty Relationship

By proactively managing these relationships I managed to maintain open our various credit lines even during period of stress (market stress, or bad press about Eisler).

I saw the opportunity to use my multi-functional role to actively manage relationships with our trading counterparties. By leading all the Credit Due Diligence meetings and developing deep trust with counterparties, I realised that we could maintain valuable credit lines even during market turmoil and existential threats to Eisler from unfounded market rumours.

Additional responsibilities — Investor Relations

I contributed to raising capital through involvement in IDD/ODD (Investment Due Diligence / Operational Due Diligence) where I presented on our Risk framework, valuation policy and on other Governance aspects of the firm. I built a solid reputation from investors/prospects (including asset management consulting firms).

My CFO/CRO role naturally led me to build our Investor Reporting. I focused on our Monthly Risk report / Newsletter as well as Opera and Hedge Platform reporting. I learnt to balance the need for transparency without giving out proprietary information. I was also involved in presenting to existing/prospect investors at IDD/ODD meetings. In these meetings I presented our control framework (e.g. portfolio valuation, risk limits and monitoring) conveying my strong beliefs that governance allows business to flourish in the long term. I inherited this value from both my time as an auditor and my years at Goldman Sachs.

However, coming from GS was not sufficient to convey a sense of Governance to our investors. This is because of reporting lines where in a Hedge Fund everyone rolls up to the founder hence independence is not guaranteed. Hence, I had to convince of my independence (e.g. telling the story about my relationship with the founder and how I gained his trust through challenging him over the years at GS).

Additional responsibilities — Treasury Management

Focusing on optimising initial margin allowed the firm to deploy more capital hence maximising the potential returns.

In the early years of the firm, as our Treasury function was not yet fully built, I focused on optimising initial margin. This comprised mostly:

  • Identifying trading inefficiencies (e.g. risk gross up) and prompting PMs to trade optimally;
  • Working with trading counterparties for a better margin approach (e.g. trade IA v risk based approach);
  • Replicating counterparties margin models (accurately or by proxy).

I realised I needed to give PMs incentives to optimise margin (hence embedding the incentive in their margin limit). I also had to engage actively with our trading counterparties so that we could progress in changing their approach.

April 1996 — May 2016 / 20 Years
Managing Director — Goldman Sachs
London (1999—2016) / Paris (1996—1999)

During my 20 year tenure with the firm:

  • I learnt about the valuation of the most complex and innovative derivatives products;
  • I managed global teams;
  • I built a market-wide reputation;
  • I influenced the market on most recent developments of derivative valuation (e.g. FVA);
  • I learnt to navigate one of the most challenging and competitive environments.
Product Controller — London — 17 years

Promotion to Managing Director — 2010. As a Managing Director in a support function, just having strong technical skills is not sufficient. It is also important to be a manager able to identify and grow young talents.

Subject Matter Expert (SME) in Derivatives Valuation

I contributed to achieve better market transparency in the prices of complex derivative instruments. My work on level 3 assets (GAAP) resulted in releasing P&L reserves of approximately $100m.

I became the go to person about derivatives valuation (especially in Fixed Income) within the Control Division. I worked with valuation service providers to set up new valuation services to enhance valuation transparency across the markets. My work on “level 3” assets directly contributed to the bottom line P&L of the firm. Learning about products and business requires spending time with traders and quants so you can learn first-hand; this is a more efficient approach than learning from colleagues who necessarily have a “control” bias.

My objective was always to be “fair” as opposed to “conservative” which set me apart in the Control division.

Driving Internal Changes

Adopting a new valuation framework put Goldman Sachs at a competitive advantage. Bottom line impact was in the tune of $100s mm of both additional revenues and savings.

I led the firm’s efforts in the Controllers Department to implement changes observed in the markets: I drove internally the adoption of OIS discounting (Overnight Index Swap) and FVA (Funding Valuation Adjustment). As a “first adopter”, this gave the firm a huge competitive advantage that materialised into both significant additional revenues and material savings. I learnt that being excessively conservative (the natural controller bias) can come in the way of growing a business. Being fair is better than being conservative but sometimes requires taking reasonable risk. My view is that this is the role of a control function to assess/evaluate to achieve the best outcome.

External Reputation

I largely contributed to the market convergence of OIS discounting and FVA. This advanced the reputation of Goldman Sachs Controllers as thought leaders both internally and externally.

I built a reputation as a SME (Subject Matter Expert) beyond the firm. As a result, I was regularly invited to discuss and present to the Banking Industry about hot topics (mostly valuation related). My external contribution also allowed for the market convergence of the methods early adopted by Goldman Sachs.

Regulatory

GS Controllers was seen by the Regulators as thought leaders.

As a Global Head, I spoke to various Regulators (e.g. UK FCA, US Fed, Japan FSA). I contributed to GS becoming the “go to” for the Regulators (especially the UK FCA) to learn about “best in class” approach on valuation questions.

Global Head

I supported multiple businesses of the Trading Division globally (especially across Rates/FX/Emerging Markets).

This experience gave me exposure to what may have been at the time nascent products/markets (e.g. inflation derivatives). Through strong engagement with the Front Office (traders/quants), I learnt first-hand from the experts.

Managing Global Teams

As a Global Head of multiple businesses, I managed teams across locations (UK, US, India, Tokyo, Hong Kong).

I created an environment where people could develop both their technical and leadership skills. My objective was to nurture and grow tomorrows’ leaders. As I was travelling regularly I understood that we can have different cultures and still have a lot in common when it comes to skills, motivation, teamwork, etc. I also came to believe strongly that cultural diversity is not just a “nice to have” but rather a business need when dealing with clients from around the world. Indeed, our firm needs to reflect the world we live in.

Promoting Diversity

Diversity is not optional but rather a business requirement.

We need to attract and retain the best people, reflect the diversity of our clients. Hence throughout the years I actively engaged in promoting an inclusive environment in my teams and beyond. I also made sure I remained aware of my own possible biases so that mistakes maybe mitigated to a large extent.

Regulatory/Financial Controller — Paris — 3 years

Held regulatory and financial control responsibilities for the Paris entity.

September 1992 — March 1996 / 4 Years
Deloitte — Paris
Audit / Financial Services

This experience allowed me to learn how to approach Corporate/Business questions with rigour and methodology.

I also learnt the importance of maintaining strong relationships with clients.

Statutory Auditor — Financial Services Division

I learnt that if you show a good understanding of the business of your clients, you will gain their respect and gather more leverage.

Non Statutory Auditor — Financial Services Division

I was appointed to several non-statutory audits (e.g. in Banking and Insurance sectors) as I felt this is where I could add more value.

Consultant — a few month detachment to the consulting arm of Deloitte

In this short experience I aimed at applying Audit rigour to consulting work.

1987 — 1992

Governance · Strategy

Governance Means Business

Early in my career, I joined Goldman Sachs in what the industry used to call a “cost centre”: product control, a back-office function, support. The kind of role that, when you mentioned it at a dinner party, prompted polite nods before the conversation moved on to someone who actually traded.

I want to challenge that framing — because thirty years later, having spent two decades at Goldman and nearly a decade as CFO and CRO of Eisler Capital, I am convinced that governance and control functions are not a cost. They are a strategic asset. And the firms that understand this are the ones that survive.

What happens when control doesn’t exist

Let me start with a structural reality that rarely gets said plainly: traders have a natural incentive to mark their positions aggressively. This is not a character flaw — it is a rational response to how they are compensated. Performance-linked pay means that a more generous mark today translates, directly, into a larger bonus. Without independent control, that incentive operates unchecked, and books drift from reality. Slowly, then suddenly.

In 2007, the collapse of the US subprime mortgage market didn’t come out of nowhere. Banks were sitting on enormous portfolios of complex structured products — mortgage-backed securities, CDOs — marked at values that bore increasingly little relationship to reality. When the market seized up and the illusion cracked, the IMF estimated total writedowns and credit losses across the global financial system at close to $1 trillion — a figure it would revise upward to $2.7 trillion by 2009. The valuation function had either failed, been ignored, or — worse — been captured by the businesses it was supposed to oversee.

More recently, in March 2021, Archegos Capital Management — a family office run by Bill Hwang — defaulted on margin calls from several global prime brokers, triggering one of the most striking governance post-mortems of recent years. What happened next was revealing: Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, both of whom had robust risk frameworks and acted swiftly, avoided material losses. Credit Suisse, whose risk controls had identified the problem as early as September 2020 but failed to act on it, lost $5.5 billion in a matter of days. Same client. Same positions. Radically different outcomes — determined entirely by the quality of governance and the willingness to act on it.

These are not edge cases. They are what governance failure looks like at scale.

The paradox of control

Here is the fundamental paradox of working in a control function: you demonstrate your value most clearly when control breaks down — but your entire job is to make sure it never does. Good governance is largely invisible. When books are clean, positions are fairly marked, and P&L is reliable, nobody notices. The noise only starts when something goes wrong.

When I joined Goldman Sachs in Product Control, my job was to produce the official P&L — the independent, verified number that reconciled against what traders believed they had made the previous day. I realised that because I had trained as an engineer, I always saw things through the perspective of data, process and control.

Most of the time, the numbers were close. But that alignment wasn’t accidental. It existed because the control was there. Remove the independent check, and you don’t get the same result with less effort — you get gradual drift, compounding mismarks, and eventually a problem that is very hard to unwind.

The days when the numbers diverged were the days that mattered most. Challenging a trader’s mark on a complex Level 3 derivative — an illiquid instrument with no observable market price — requires technical depth and a certain kind of institutional courage. You are, in effect, telling a senior revenue generator that their position is worth less than they believe. That conversation only ends well if you are right, rigorous, and — crucially — trusted.

Control and support: two sides of the same function

This brings me to something I feel strongly about, and which I think is widely misunderstood about governance functions: the job is not only to control. It is also to support. The most effective people in these roles understand that the two are not in tension — they are mutually reinforcing.

At Goldman, my work was not purely defensive. I championed the early adoption of OIS discounting and FVA — methodologies that were becoming market standards but which few firms had yet implemented. Being a first mover generated hundreds of millions of dollars in additional revenues and risk management savings for the firm. That came from a control function. From someone whose job description was supposedly to check numbers and push back on marks.

The practical implication of this balance is significant. Senior trading heads are much more receptive to being challenged on a control issue when they also know you add genuine commercial value. Trust is the currency. If you have spent months working alongside a desk, helping them think through valuation methodology, flagging competitive dynamics, and solving real problems — then the moment you push back on a mark carries weight. They know you are not just a bureaucrat ticking a box. You are a partner who has earned the right to be heard.

The control function only works at full strength when it is also a trusted partner to the business. One without the other is either captured or ignored.

The Goldman model: from cost centre to federation

What Goldman Sachs understood — and I believe understood better than most — was that the long-term health of the firm depended on the integrity of its governance culture. Lloyd Blankfein consistently referred to the “federation” in his speeches: the idea that the support and control functions were not subordinate to the front office, but equal partners in the firm’s success.

This wasn’t just rhetoric. It showed up in how people were promoted, how careers were built, and how the firm made decisions. In 2010, when I was promoted to Managing Director, something notable happened in my promotion class: for the first time, the number of MDs promoted from the federation — control, risk, finance, legal, compliance — exceeded the number promoted from the front office. That was a deliberate signal. It said: this firm values governance. It invests in it. It promotes the people who do it well.

What I built at Eisler

When I joined Eisler Capital at inception in 2016, I had the opportunity to build governance from scratch — and to test, in a founder-led environment, whether the Goldman model could translate to a fast-scaling hedge fund.

In the hedge fund world, governance is not just internally important — it is a fundraising asset. Institutional investors — pension funds, endowments, sovereign wealth funds — run rigorous operational due diligence before allocating capital. They want to know that a fund’s P&L is independently verified, that its valuation methodology is robust, that its risk limits are real and enforced, and that there is genuine separation between the investment function and the control environment.

Over nine years, as we grew from $700 million to $4.5 billion in AUM, I sat in those ODD meetings, presenting our governance framework. Consistently, investors told us our controls were among the strongest they had seen. That credibility was not incidental to our growth — it was a direct contributor to it.

Strong governance doesn’t just protect you from disaster. It opens doors.

What I would tell my younger self

If you are early in a governance, control, risk, or finance career, I want to be direct with you: do not let anyone make you feel like a second-class citizen because you are not in the front office. The firms that treat governance as a cost centre are the ones you read about in post-mortems.

Build the technical depth to challenge anyone on substance. Build the commercial awareness to understand what the business actually needs. And build the relationships that mean people want to hear from you — not just when you are delivering bad news, but because you make them better at their job.

That combination — rigour, judgement, partnership — is rare. And it is, in my experience, what separates the functions that genuinely add value from those that merely process paperwork.

Governance means business. Not as a slogan. As a lived reality.

Artificial Intelligence · Operations

A Few Thoughts About Leveraging AI to Boost Support Functions

Throughout my career, I have tried to take a consistent approach to technology: follow it closely, understand what it actually does, and adopt it when it solves a real business problem — not because it is generating excitement. That discipline shaped how we worked at Goldman Sachs and at Eisler Capital, and it is the lens through which I approach AI. I am a finance and risk professional who has spent thirty years building and leading support functions, and from that vantage point, I think the opportunity is significant — provided we approach it with the same rigour we apply to everything else.

The problem AI is well-suited to solve

Support functions — finance, risk, fund accounting, compliance, operations — share a structural characteristic: they consume a disproportionate amount of skilled people’s time on tasks that are high-volume, rules-based, and largely repetitive. Reconciliations. Exception flagging. Variance commentary. Report generation. Data validation across systems.

This is not a trivial observation. When I was leading the fund accounting function at Eisler, two firm-wide initiatives shaped how the business operated: the automation of the pass-through expense workflow, and a shift in P&L production from a London-close T+1 cycle to a live, multi-regional intraday environment. Both were decisions I was closely involved in driving — from design through to delivery. That transition required significant process redesign and technology investment — and the payoff was real: portfolio managers gained same-day visibility into their P&L, which materially improved front-office risk awareness and decision-making. We did this without AI, through disciplined process engineering. But looking back, the volume of data matching, reconciliation, and exception handling involved in that build is exactly the kind of work where AI-driven automation would have compressed timelines and freed the team to focus on the judgement-intensive work that actually required their expertise.

The broader data points the same way. According to L.E.K. Consulting’s 2025 CFO survey, around 60% of CFOs now believe AI will be among the most impactful technologies in their function over the coming years — up from roughly 50% in 2024. PwC found that AI-enabled procure-to-pay workflows are cutting cycle times by up to 80%. These are not marginal efficiency gains. They are structural changes in what a lean, high-performing support function can look like.

Freeing talent to focus on judgement

The most important benefit of AI in support functions is not cost reduction — though that follows. It is the reallocation of human capital toward work that actually requires human judgement.

In my years running Product Control at Goldman Sachs, the most valuable work my teams did was not producing the P&L. It was what happened when the P&L diverged from the trader’s estimate — the investigation, the challenge, the conversation with the desk about why a Level 3 position was being marked aggressively. That work required technical depth, commercial awareness, and institutional courage. It could not be automated. But it was constantly competing for bandwidth with the mechanics of producing the number in the first place.

AI changes that equation. If the routine data processing, the reconciliation loops, and the first-pass exception flagging can be handled by well-designed models, the human in the loop shifts from operator to analyst — from producing outputs to interrogating them. That is a significant upgrade in the strategic contribution of the function. And it is precisely the direction of travel that firms competing for institutional capital need to be heading.

There is, however, an apparent tension in this argument that is worth naming directly. If AI handles the routine work, how does the next generation of analysts develop the judgement to oversee it? The answer is that AI changes what people do — it does not replace the need for people who genuinely know what they are doing. Oversight is only as good as the person providing it. A professional who has never reconciled a complex position, never challenged a trader’s mark, never traced an exception through to its source, is not equipped to judge whether an AI’s output is right or subtly wrong. The apprenticeship still matters. What changes is that it should increasingly focus on the hard work — the investigation, the challenge, the judgement call — rather than the mechanics of producing the number in the first place.

Making control more robust

The second dimension is control quality. AI does not just automate — it can monitor continuously, at a scale and consistency that human teams cannot match.

When the risk framework at Eisler was being built from scratch — a firm-wide priority I led from the outset — one of the central design challenges was ensuring the limit structure was genuinely live, not one that was checked at end of day after the risk had already been taken. The firm invested heavily in real-time risk monitoring, with price feeds, Greeks, VaR, and PM-level exposure dashboards updated intraday. The goal was to ensure that by the time I sat down with portfolio managers in the weekly risk review, the data was granular enough to drive real decisions on sizing, hedging, and unwind.

AI extends this capability significantly. Anomaly detection across large, complex datasets — identifying unusual patterns in position marks, flagging outliers in expense flows, detecting correlations in risk exposures that a human reviewer might miss — is precisely where machine learning models outperform manual oversight. The control function becomes not just more efficient but more comprehensive. Harder to game. More credible to investors and regulators.

Governance as a competitive advantage — amplified

In my article “Governance Means Business”, I argued that governance is not a cost centre but a strategic asset — that strong controls are a fundraising advantage in the institutional capital markets. AI extends that argument further.

Institutional investors running operational due diligence are becoming increasingly sophisticated. They are not just asking whether controls exist — they are asking whether those controls are scalable, whether they are real-time, and whether the governance infrastructure can grow with the business without proportional headcount growth. A firm that can demonstrate AI-augmented controls — systematic anomaly detection, automated reconciliation with human sign-off, live risk dashboards — is telling a very different story than one that relies entirely on manual processes. It signals institutional maturity. It signals scalability. And in a world where investors are increasingly focused on operational risk, it translates directly into capital.

The risks — and why they matter more in our world

None of this is unconditional. I think the risks of poorly implemented AI in support functions are substantial — and in some ways more acute here than in other parts of the business. Let me be specific.

Erosion of judgement — and this is the risk I think about most. If junior professionals in support functions spend their formative years reviewing AI outputs rather than producing and interrogating analysis themselves, we risk hollowing out the very capability we are trying to augment. The next generation of CFOs and CROs needs to have built the judgement to know when a model is wrong — and that judgement comes from having done the work manually first. AI should compress the time spent on mechanics, not eliminate the apprenticeship. A function staffed by people who have only ever reviewed outputs is not a stronger function. It is a more fragile one.

Data quality. AI models are only as reliable as the data they are trained and run on. In finance and risk functions, data integrity is not a nice-to-have — it is the foundation on which every output rests. Garbage in, garbage out is not a cliché here; it is a governance risk. Before any AI layer is introduced, the underlying data architecture needs to be clean, consistent, and well-governed. This is harder than it sounds in legacy environments.

Hallucination and unreliable outputs. Generative AI in particular has a well-documented tendency to produce confident-sounding outputs that are simply wrong. In a P&L commentary, a regulatory report, or a risk summary, that is not an acceptable failure mode. Any deployment in a control environment needs robust human review at the output stage — not as a bureaucratic layer, but as a genuine check. The model flags; the human decides.

Finally, governance of the AI itself. Who owns the models? Who validates them? Who decides when an output can be acted upon without human review? These are not IT questions. They are governance questions — and they belong at the CFO, CRO and COO level, not buried in a technology team. In a regulated environment, model risk governance is already a well-established discipline. Firms that extend that rigour to their AI deployments will be in a fundamentally stronger position — with investors, regulators, and internally.

Where I land

I am not yet at the point where I can speak from deep operational experience of deploying AI at scale in a support function. But I know these functions intimately — what they produce, where they struggle, and where the value lies. And I am convinced that the firms which get this right will have a genuine competitive advantage: leaner teams doing higher-quality work, controls that are more comprehensive and more credible, and a governance story that resonates with the most sophisticated allocators in the market.

The question of who governs the AI — who owns the models, who validates the outputs, who decides when a machine can be trusted to act — is not a technology question. It belongs at the CFO, CRO and COO level. Those who treat it as such will build something genuinely stronger. Those who delegate it will eventually find out why they shouldn’t have.

AI changes the work. It does not change the standard.

Artificial Intelligence · Governance

AI Needs a Strong Governance Framework

Throughout my career, I have operated by a simple principle: technology must serve a clear strategy — not substitute for one. When I was building the governance infrastructure at Eisler Capital from the ground up — risk frameworks, valuation controls, fund accounting — we made deliberate choices about which technologies to adopt and how to govern them. That discipline shaped everything we built. And it is the same discipline I think the industry needs to apply to artificial intelligence, now, before the pace of adoption runs ahead of the frameworks designed to manage it.

AI is not a rupture with what came before. For firms that have consistently leveraged technology to sharpen their operations, it is the latest chapter in that discipline — not a fundamentally different one. What makes AI distinctive is that it puts tool-building directly in the hands of non-technical professionals. A portfolio manager, a risk analyst, or a compliance officer can now build and deploy their own AI-powered solutions without writing a line of code. That is a profound shift — and it is precisely why governance matters more, not less. Without a firm-wide framework, the natural result is a patchwork of disconnected tools, each solving a local problem but collectively creating new ones.

In my article ‘A Few Thoughts About Leveraging AI to Boost Support Functions’, I argued that AI offers genuine and significant opportunity for support functions — automating the routine, freeing talent for judgement-intensive work, and making controls more comprehensive. I stand by that. But opportunity without governance is just risk in disguise. So let me turn to the harder question: what does a serious AI governance framework actually look like for a hedge fund?

These are my views — written from thirty years of building and leading governance functions, first at Goldman Sachs and then at Eisler Capital.

AIMA’s 2025 research found that 95% of alternative investment managers are now using generative AI in some capacity, up from 86% in 2023. At the same time, the same research flagged a persistent governance gap: half of fund managers running less than $1 billion in AUM reported having no restrictions at all on how their staff use generative AI tools. That is not a technology problem. It is a governance failure waiting to materialise.

Start where the regulators start

The natural starting point for any governance framework is the regulatory baseline. What do the regulators actually require?

In the UK, the FCA has been unambiguous: it will not introduce AI-specific rules, preferring instead its principles-based, outcomes-focused approach. Senior manager accountability under SM&CR and governance and systems-and-controls requirements under SYSC form the core regulatory framework that applies to AI in hedge funds. The message is clear: firms must evidence who owns each AI-enabled process, what approvals were obtained before deployment, what controls apply, and how performance is monitored and escalated. Accountability cannot be delegated to an algorithm. SM&CR makes that explicit.

The approach in Dubai is strikingly consistent — though perhaps unsurprising, given that the DFSA has long aligned itself closely to international regulatory standards, particularly those of the FCA. The DFSA’s Chief Executive has confirmed that the DFSA is not planning to introduce rules to regulate AI specifically, but will treat it as a risk factor that firms must manage within their existing regulatory obligations. As of mid-2025, 52% of DIFC-regulated firms are actively using AI, up from 33% in 2024, with the DFSA actively building out its supervisory framework. Across the UAE more broadly, joint guidelines on enabling technologies — issued by the Central Bank of the UAE, the Securities and Commodities Authority, the DFSA, and the FSRA — emphasise that financial institutions must have adequate governance, accountability, and risk management frameworks in place. Non-binding for now, but the direction of travel is clear.

The regulatory baseline, in short, tells you the minimum: clear ownership, documented controls, senior accountability, escalation routes. It is a necessary foundation. It is not sufficient.

What a serious framework looks like beyond the baseline

This is where genuine thought leadership separates itself from compliance box-ticking. A firm that builds its AI governance framework to satisfy the regulator and stops there has missed the point. The firms that will build durable competitive advantage are the ones that go further.

The pillars of a serious framework fall into two distinct categories. The first — governance and policy — sets the rules: it defines who owns what, which standards apply, and how compliance is enforced. The second — operations and resilience — defines the operational environment without which those rules cannot function in practice. Data quality, model configuration and training, the preservation of human judgement, and contingency planning are not governance afterthoughts. They are the conditions that determine whether governance works at all. Both categories are necessary. Neither is sufficient without the other.

Ownership and the role of the Federation. At Goldman Sachs, we used the term “Federation” to describe the support and control functions — finance, risk, compliance, legal, operations and technology. The Federation was not subordinate to the front office; it was a partner to it. That framing matters enormously in the context of AI governance. In many firms, technology teams build the tools, the front office uses them, and the non-technology parts of the Federation are left trying to understand outputs they had no hand in designing, and often no visibility into how they were built. That is the wrong model.

AI governance belongs with senior non-investment leadership — not buried in a technology team. In most hedge funds, it is the COO who is best placed to chair that collaboration. The Federation defines the standards and owns the accountability. Technology validates the models. The front office provides the commercial context. Each plays a role that the others cannot replicate.

A firm-wide policy, not a patchwork. One of the most significant risks I see in how firms are approaching AI is the proliferation of disconnected tools — individual teams building their own solutions, often without coordination, and certainly without consistent standards. A firm-wide AI policy is not bureaucracy for its own sake. It is the only way to ensure consistency, auditability, and scalability. That policy needs to address, at a minimum: which AI tools are approved for use and by whom; how models are validated before deployment and monitored after; how proprietary data is protected — including the risk of inadvertent leakage through external large language model platforms; and what the escalation and override process looks like when an AI output is challenged.

A related dimension that deserves explicit attention: third-party vendor oversight. Many hedge funds rely on external platforms — prime broker tools, administrator systems, risk analytics providers — that are increasingly embedding AI into their services. Firms need to augment their vendor due diligence processes accordingly: understanding how AI is being used in the services they receive, what data is being ingested, and whether appropriate contractual protections are in place to prevent proprietary information from being used to train third-party models.

Data quality and model configuration as operational prerequisites. AI models are only as reliable as the data they are trained and run on. In a hedge fund context — where the data environment spans prime broker feeds, administrator figures, internal P&L systems, and risk infrastructure — data integrity is not a given. It is something that has to be actively maintained. Before an AI layer is introduced into any critical process, the underlying data architecture needs to be clean, consistent, and well-governed.

Preserving human judgement — and building it in the first place. If junior professionals in support functions spend their formative years reviewing AI outputs rather than producing and interrogating analysis themselves, we risk hollowing out the very capability we are trying to augment. Good oversight requires people who genuinely know what they are doing — who have reconciled complex positions, challenged aggressive marks, traced exceptions to their source. That knowledge comes from doing the work, not from reviewing a model’s summary of it. AI should compress the time spent on mechanics. It should not eliminate the apprenticeship.

Contingency planning. A point that is often absent from AI governance discussions: what happens when the technology fails? If a firm has automated its reconciliation process to the point where no one remembers how to do it manually, it has not built efficiency. It has built fragility.

A governance committee to ensure compliance. Defining a policy is one thing. Ensuring it is actually complied with is another — and this is where many frameworks fall short. They are written, filed, and largely forgotten until something goes wrong. The answer is a dedicated governance committee, chaired by the COO and drawing its membership from senior non-investment leadership. Its remit: review that the tasks set out in the policy have been performed, assess the results, and ensure the policy remains fit for purpose. Given how fast AI is evolving, the policy itself should be on the committee’s agenda for formal reassessment at least once a year.

Governance of AI is a competitive signal

I made the argument in “Governance Means Business” that strong governance is a fundraising asset. AI governance is becoming part of that signal. AIMA’s research found that 60% of institutional investors would be more likely to allocate to a hedge fund that makes meaningful investment in AI — but the same investors are also asking harder questions about how that AI is governed. A firm that can demonstrate structured model ownership, validated outputs, and clear human accountability is telling a very different story than one that has simply enabled AI tools across the firm and called it a strategy.

Where to start

For a hedge fund without a mature AI governance framework, the path forward is not complicated, but it does require deliberate leadership. Start with the regulatory baseline — understand what SM&CR, SYSC, and your local regulator already require, and map your AI deployments against those obligations. Build a firm-wide policy that covers approved tools, model validation, data governance, vendor oversight, and human escalation, and assign compliance oversight to a dedicated governance committee. Establish clear ownership with senior non-investment leadership. Bring in technology and the front office as partners, not afterthoughts. And invest in the people — not just in training them to use AI, but in developing the foundational judgement that makes oversight meaningful.

AIMA’s practical guidance on AI governance, developed with K&L Gates, recommends a structured approach built around six elements: employee training programmes; an acceptable use policy; a formal governance committee; a model risk management framework; regular stress testing and scenario analysis; and clear disclosure obligations. This maps closely to the framework I have described — and it is encouraging that the industry body is pushing in the same direction.

The governance frameworks that financial firms have built over decades — for risk, for valuation, for compliance — did not emerge fully formed. They were built iteratively, tested under stress, and refined over time. AI governance will follow the same path. The firms that start building seriously now will have a structural advantage over those that treat it as a technology project to be handled later.

AI changes the tools. It does not change the standard.

1 SM&CR (Senior Managers & Certification Regime): the FCA framework that requires firms to assign clear accountability for business activities to named senior individuals, ensuring responsibility cannot be diffused or delegated away.

2 SYSC (Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls): the FCA sourcebook that sets out requirements for firms to have robust governance, oversight structures, and internal controls.

AI · Governance · Visual Guide

AI Governance Framework for Alternative Investment Managers

This visual guide accompanies the article AI Needs a Strong Governance Framework and sets out the key pillars of an effective AI governance framework, how inputs drive policy, what that policy requires in practice, and who owns each activity.

Didier Breant · 2026

Leadership · Career

The Value of a Mentor

If I could give one piece of advice to my younger self at the very start of my career, it would be this: understand the value of a mentor. Not because it’s a trendy concept or something HR puts on development plans, but because the right mentor can fundamentally change the trajectory of your career.

But here’s the truth: when I started, I didn’t have a mentor — and I didn’t even think about it. I assumed careers progressed through hard work, technical competence, and a bit of luck. Mentorship felt like something that happened to other people, not something I needed to worry about.

Years later, I realised how wrong that assumption was.

How Mentorship Begins Without You Realising It

In my early years, I focused entirely on doing the job in front of me. I was determined, disciplined, and eager to prove myself. I built relationships with colleagues, worked long hours, and tried to absorb as much as I could. I thought that was enough.

Then, one day, someone casually mentioned that a senior colleague had become “a mentor” to me. I remember being surprised. I hadn’t labelled the relationship that way. We simply had good conversations. He challenged my thinking. He helped me step back from daily issues that felt overwhelming. He encouraged me to take opportunities I might have ignored.

Only then did I realise something important: you build professional relationships, and naturally one of them becomes stronger than the others. That’s how mentorship often begins.

It’s not formal. It’s not forced. It’s not something you “apply for.”

It grows organically — long before you recognise it.

The challenge for many young professionals is that they don’t know what they don’t know. When you’re early in your career, you’re focused on mastering the technical aspects of the job, understanding the systems, learning the products, and proving your value. You’re not thinking about long-term navigation or strategic choices. And because of that, you may not realise that you’re missing guidance.

What You Lose Without a Mentor

Without a mentor, you lack perspective when daily issues feel overwhelming. You may confuse urgency with importance. You risk making career decisions based on emotion or frustration. You may miss opportunities simply because no one nudged you toward them. You don’t have someone who has “seen the movie before” and can warn you about the plot twists.

And here’s a key point: talking to friends and trusted colleagues is valuable, but it is not mentoring. Friends empathise. Colleagues share experiences. But a mentor provides something different — distance, perspective, and judgement shaped by experience.

Why You Shouldn’t Go Looking for a Mentor

The dilemma is simple: You need a mentor long before you realise you need one — but you shouldn’t obsess about finding one.

The resolution lies in understanding how mentorship actually happens. You don’t choose a mentor the way you choose a course or a project. Instead, you focus on doing excellent work, building relationships, and expanding your network. And through that process, the right person naturally emerges.

This is why you shouldn’t obsess about “finding a mentor.” Be aware of the value, yes — but don’t force it. When you do good work, when you show curiosity, when you build trust, your network expands. And within that network, someone will stand out. Someone whose judgement you value. Someone who challenges you. Someone who has walked the path before you. That person becomes your mentor — often without either of you formally acknowledging it.

What a Great Mentor Actually Looks Like

A mentor is someone who has extensive, relevant experience; is senior enough to offer perspective; has judgement you genuinely trust; inspires you; makes you feel comfortable being honest; challenges you constructively; and helps you think long-term, not just about your next task.

And here’s another important truth: you will have several mentors throughout your career. As you grow, change roles, or move countries, your needs evolve. The mentor who was perfect when you were a graduate may not be the one you need when you become a manager.

A good mentor helps you optimise your career path because you’re not navigating blindly. They help you avoid mistakes they made themselves. They help you see opportunities you might overlook. They help you understand that the road to success is often bumpy — but with guidance, you avoid the major potholes.

Conclusion

Looking back, I realise how much time I lost simply because I didn’t have someone to help me zoom out. Someone to say, “This issue feels big today, but it won’t matter in six months.” Someone to help me see beyond the immediate tasks. Someone to challenge my assumptions and broaden my perspective.

A mentor doesn’t solve your problems. They help you understand them. They help you navigate uncertainty. They help you grow faster, with more clarity and less unnecessary stress.

So here’s my advice: Be aware of the value of a mentor — but don’t obsess about finding one. Focus on doing excellent work. Expand your network. Let relationships develop naturally.

The right mentor may already be in your orbit — you just haven’t recognised them yet.
